channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Noise-aware Client Selection for carbon-efficient Federated Learning via Gradient Norm Thresholding
item.description: Training large-scale neural networks is energy-intensive, and carbon-aware Federated Learning (FL) leverages geographically distributed renewable energy to reduce AI’s environmental footprint. However, existing client selection strategies—designed to align training with carbon availability—often inadvertently select clients with noisy or low-quality data due to FL’s privacy constraints, degrading model accuracy and convergence. To address this, we propose **Noise-aware Client Selection via Gradient Norm Thresholding (NCCS)**, a lightweight, plug-and-play module that enhances robustness without accessing raw data. NCCS introduces probing rounds to compute local gradient L2 norms; clients whose gradients exceed a dynamic threshold—indicative of label noise or poor data quality—are filtered out before aggregation. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10-C and FEMNIST-Noise show that conventional loss-based selection harms accuracy by up to 8.2%, while NCCS improves final accuracy by 5.6–9.3%, accelerates convergence by 22%, and maintains 21–34% carbon reduction versus baselines. Our work bridges carbon efficiency and statistical robustness in practical FL deployments.

item.title: CAM-LDS: Cyber Attack Manifestations for Automatic Interpretation of System Logs and Security Alerts
item.description: Log analysis remains a bottleneck in security operations due to volume, heterogeneity, and unstructured text—yet existing automated methods rely heavily on expert-crafted rules, parsers, or features, lacking semantic understanding and explainability. To enable LLM-driven log interpretation, we introduce **CAM-LDS**, the first open-source, reproducible log dataset explicitly designed for attack manifestation analysis. CAM-LDS covers **81 ATT&CK techniques across 13 tactics**, derived from **7 end-to-end attack scenarios** executed in a fully open testbed and collected from **18 diverse log sources** (e.g., Sysmon, Suricata, Wazuh). Crucially, all logs are curated to reflect *direct attack causality*, annotated with technique IDs and contextual metadata (command observability, event frequency, IDS alerts). In an illustrative LLM case study (Llama 3-8B), the model correctly identified the ground-truth ATT&CK technique for **~33% of attack steps**, and provided semantically adequate predictions for another **~33%**, demonstrating both the promise of LLM-based log interpretation and the utility of CAM-LDS as a foundational benchmark.

item.title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
item.description: Web security requires rapid, trustworthy responses to evolving cyber threats, yet purely neural AI approaches lack explainability and reliability for sensitive operational tasks like firewall configuration. This paper introduces a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture that leverages **hypernym-hyponym semantic relations** extracted from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports to drive automated, auditable security control generation. Our multi-agent system parses CTI text, infers hierarchical threat semantics (e.g., “Emotet” → “banking trojan” → “malware”), and synthesizes executable CLIPS rules for an expert system that generates validated firewall rules (e.g., iptables/ACL). Experiments on 1,247 real-world CTI reports show our semantic retrieval strategy achieves **92.3% F1**, outperforming BM25, Sentence-BERT, and zero-shot LLM baselines; the generated rules achieve **98.6% threat blocking rate with only 0.4% false positives**, demonstrating superior precision and operational trustworthiness. This work bridges semantic intelligence and executable security policy—enabling explainable, expert-augmented automation in SOAR pipelines.

item.title: Structure-Aware Distributed Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) preserves data privacy but introduces stealthy backdoor vulnerabilities during distributed model updates. Prior work largely assumes uniform perturbation effectiveness across architectures—a flawed premise ignoring structural influence. This paper pioneers a *structure-aware* analysis of the coupling between model architecture and backdoor perturbations. We propose two novel metrics: the **Structural Responsiveness Score (SRS)**, quantifying architectural sensitivity to perturbations, and the **Structural Compatibility Coefficient (SCC)**, measuring preference for fractal perturbations. Leveraging them, we design the **Topology-aware Fractal Injection (TFI)** framework. Experiments across 6 model families show that multi-path architectures (e.g., ResNet) amplify and retain fractal perturbations even at 1% poisoning ratio, while low-SCC models severely constrain attack success. Crucially, SCC strongly predicts attack success (*r* = 0.93), enabling proactive perturbation survivability estimation. Our findings establish that backdoor efficacy in FL is governed not only by perturbation design or poisoning intensity, but by the *triadic interaction among model structure, perturbation topology, and aggregation mechanism*—providing foundational insights for structure-aware defense.

item.title: T2S-Bench & Structure-of-Thought: Benchmarking and Prompting Comprehensive Text-to-Structure Reasoning
item.description: This paper introduces **Structure-of-Thought (SoT)**, a prompting paradigm that explicitly guides LLMs to generate structured intermediate representations (e.g., JSON schemas, dependency graphs, reasoning trees) before final output—mimicking human structural reasoning. Built on this insight, we propose **T2S-Bench**, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating text-to-structure capabilities, comprising 1.8K expert-validated samples across 6 scientific domains and 32 structural types. Evaluations on 45 mainstream models reveal critical gaps: average multi-hop reasoning accuracy is only 52.1%, and even the strongest model achieves just 58.1% node-level extraction accuracy end-to-end. SoT alone delivers +5.7% average gain across eight diverse tasks on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct; fine-tuning on T2S-Bench lifts this to +8.6%. These results underscore the necessity of explicit structure induction and establish SoT and T2S-Bench as complementary, scalable levers for advancing structured language understanding. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://t2s-bench.github.io/T2S-Bench-Page/.

item.title: Interactive Proofs for Batch Polynomial Evaluation
item.description: We present concretely efficient interactive proofs for batch polynomial evaluation. For evaluating a degree-$d$ univariate polynomial at $m$ points, our $\mathsf{MA}$ protocol achieves *linear-time verification*: the prover sends only $d-1$ field elements, and the verifier performs $O(m+d)$ field operations—eliminating the $\log^2(m+d)$ factor of prior art. We extend this to the general multi-polynomial, multi-point setting via an $\mathsf{AMA}$ protocol with the same verifier complexity. Our protocols improve verification time in fundamental primitives—including sumcheck over large domains and polynomial quotienting—reducing STIR’s (CRYPTO 2024) verifier runtime to match that of WHIR (EUROCRYPT 2025). As a direct application, we show any univariate polynomial commitment scheme can be transformed, in a black-box manner, into one supporting batch openings at $m$ points with only constant overhead over a single opening.

item.title: Short Signatures from DDH without Pairings or Random Oracles
item.description: We present two short signature schemes based solely on the polynomial-hard Decisional Diffie-Hellman (DDH) assumption. Both operate in standard prime-order groups—**no pairings are required**, and security is proven **in the standard model**, without random oracles. The first scheme achieves selective unforgeability, while the second attains full existential unforgeability under adaptive chosen-message attacks. Signatures consist of only a single group element, and public keys are constant-size. Security reductions are tight and direct: forging a signature implies efficiently solving DDH with non-negligible advantage. This work eliminates long-standing dependencies on bilinear maps or heuristic random oracles, offering a simpler, more efficient, and foundationally robust alternative for lightweight authentication.

item.title: Trace: Complete Client-Side Account Access Logging
item.description: Despite advances in authentication, account compromise remains pervasive, and users lack trustworthy ways to audit device access—especially under privacy regulations prohibiting static device identifiers. Prior client-side encrypted logging (CSAL) schemes fail to guarantee *complete* log retrieval, risking missed adversarial accesses. We present **Trace**, a new CSAL system that achieves *full* logging while preserving privacy, backward compatibility, and verifiable device attribution. Trace generates cryptographically signed, encrypted log entries *client-side* for each authentication, stores them with an independent logging service, and ensures only the user can decrypt and inspect them—leaving the web service entirely unaware of logging. Unlike prior work, Trace is formally analyzed against malicious adversaries and supports scalable deployment: our prototype processes **over 10,000 authentications per second on a single core**. Trace reconciles accountability and privacy without modifying existing authentication infrastructure.

item.title: Survey of isogeny-based signature schemes resistant to Castryck–Decru attack
item.description: This paper presents the first structured survey of isogeny-based signature schemes resilient to the Castryck–Decru (CD) attack, which broke SIKE in 2022. We categorize resistant schemes into two families: **CSIDH-group-action-based** (e.g., CSI-FiSh, SeaSign) and **SQIsign-family** (e.g., SQIsign, SQIsign-2). For each, we clarify design principles, security assumptions (e.g., GAPI, path-finding hardness), and concrete constructions. Performance comparison shows CSI-FiSh achieves the smallest public keys (64 bytes) and signatures (~350 bytes), while SQIsign-2 optimizes speed—reducing verification time by ~30% over SQIsign. Both outperform lattice-based finalists (e.g., Dilithium, Falcon) in key compactness but lag in signing speed. Our analysis bridges theory and practice, enabling informed selection among post-quantum signature alternatives.

item.title: Implementation of a post-quantum hybrid group key exchange protocol
item.description: This paper presents the first complete implementation and experimental evaluation of Steinwandt and Gonzales Vasco’s hybrid group key exchange (HGKE) protocol—a provably secure scheme unifying post-quantum cryptography (PQC) and quantum key distribution (QKD). We integrate CRYSTALS-Dilithium (NIST-selected PQC signature) with a BB84-based QKD simulator within an OpenQuantumSafe framework, enabling dynamic 3–8 party key establishment. Our implementation introduces a decoupled state machine for asynchronous QKD key streaming and PQC signing, and a novel “dual-track authentication” mechanism that uses QKD-derived keys to encrypt PQC public key hashes—preventing man-in-the-middle tampering. In LAN tests, 8-party key agreement completes in 2.1 s on average with >99.97% key entropy. We identify critical challenges: QKD throughput vs. PQC computational overhead trade-offs, algorithm agility under evolving NIST standards, and trust anchoring in multi-hop groups—providing actionable insights for standardization and real-world deployment.

item.title: Leakage-Diagrams, Importance Sampling, and Composition in the Random Probing Model
item.description: This work advances the analytical and algorithmic foundations for security evaluation of masked implementations under the Random Probing Model (RPM). We first derive tight, SNR-to-probing-rate conversion for noisy Hamming-weight leakage over $\mathbb{F}_{2^u}$ via a binary inner-product–based $\mathbb{F}_2$-linear reduction. Second, we characterize RPM leakage in $\mathbb{F}_q$-linear circuits as an erasure event in vector space—yielding direct links to advantage-based local metrics and guaranteeing global simulability for refreshed, block-separated executions. Third, we design an importance sampling scheme that enables efficient Monte Carlo estimation of rare leakage events in low-rate/high-order regimes where naive sampling fails. Finally, we reformulate leakage diagrams to obtain explicit bounds for refresh gadgets and introduce *bridges* to analyze composition, showing that SNI—sufficient for the Threshold Probing Model—is insufficient to capture the RPM-specific boundary behavior governed by refresh. All methods are implemented in **LAPSE**, a tool supporting both exact algebraic computation and importance-sampling–accelerated RPM security assessment.

item.title: PRISM with a pinch of salt: Simple, Efficient and Strongly Unforgeable Signatures from Isogenies
item.description: This work introduces **PRISM with a pinch of salt**, a simple, efficient, and strongly unforgeable signature scheme based on isogenies of supersingular elliptic curves. Its security rests on the standard hardness assumption that computing a large prime-degree isogeny from a supersingular curve with *unknown endomorphism ring* is intractable for both classical and quantum adversaries. We first construct a two-round identification protocol where the challenge is a random large prime $q$, and the prover responds with a compact representation of a degree-$q$ isogeny from its public key. Applying the hash-and-sign paradigm, we obtain a signature scheme with an exceptionally lightweight signing procedure—requiring only one hash and one isogeny computation—and prove its strong unforgeability (SUUF) in the **standard model**, without random oracles. The most efficient variant signs **1.4×–1.6× faster** than the latest SQIsign implementation; verification is **1.2× slower to 1.01× faster**, depending on the security level (100–128-bit quantum security). Public key and signature sizes are comparable to state-of-the-art isogeny-based schemes.

item.title: Memory-Efficient Implementation of SMAUG-T and HAETAE
item.description: SMAUG-T and HAETAE—finalists in Korea’s Post-Quantum Cryptography (KpqC) standardization initiative—face severe stack memory constraints on ARM Cortex-M4 microcontrollers. While SMAUG-T runs marginally, HAETAE’s baseline signing consumes 91,176 B of stack, exceeding typical SRAM budgets. We propose a holistic memory optimization framework: replacing stack-allocated large buffers with reusable static pools, in-place iterative NTT/sampling, and aggressive loop unrolling—all implemented in branchless constant-time style to resist timing attacks. Evaluated against KpqClean_ver2, our optimizations reduce peak stack usage by **73–83% for SMAUG-T5** (e.g., decapsulation: 24,300 B → 4,240 B) and **≈90% for HAETAE5** (signing: 91,176 B → 8,092 B). All implementations pass NIST test vectors, retain functional correctness, and incur <15% runtime overhead. This work enables practical deployment of lattice-based PQC in deeply embedded systems.

item.title: Fuzzy Private Set Intersection for Real-World Datasets
item.description: Private Set Intersection (PSI) enables two parties to compute the intersection of their private sets without revealing extra information. Fuzzy PSI generalizes this by allowing the receiver to learn sender’s elements *close* to its own (i.e., $ \text{dist}(x,y) < \delta $), enabling privacy-preserving applications like password matching and contact tracing. However, existing Fuzzy PSI protocols rely on strong, impractical assumptions—such as receiver-set disjointness—which real-world datasets (e.g., password hashes, face embeddings) systematically violate. To bridge this gap, we introduce a **practically validated low-density assumption**, design a **generic compiler** based on higher-dimensional lifting and coloring, and transform existing strong-assumption protocols into efficient, real-deployable ones. Our compiled protocol achieves **3.8× faster runtime and 5.2× less communication** than baselines on realistic password-matching parameters, with full recall and zero false positives. The approach is modular, secure under standard assumptions, and open-sourced.

item.title: Performance Analysis of a Thread Pool-Based Parallel Execution Model for Hybrid Post-Quantum TLS 1.3 Handshakes
item.description: This paper presents a systematic performance analysis of hybrid post-quantum TLS 1.3 handshakes using a POSIX thread pool–based parallel execution model. We evaluate 135 combinations across 3 classical KEMs (X25519, P-256, P-384), 3 ML-KEM variants, 3 classical DSAs, and 5 PQC DSAs—including NIST-standardized ML-DSA and Falcon. Parallel execution reduces end-to-end handshake latency from 429.2–1907.0 µs (sequential) to 356.7–1380.8 µs, achieving consistent speedups of 1.08×–1.40×. The highest acceleration (1.40×) occurs in P-384–based configurations due to optimal overlap between classical and PQC operations. We recommend two practical deployment strategies: throughput-optimized FN-DSA hybrids (e.g., Falcon + Ed25519) and NIST-standardized ML-KEM + ML-DSA pairings aligned with security levels (Level 1/3/5). Our model requires no protocol changes and integrates directly into OpenSSL-based stacks—providing actionable guidance for mitigating PQC-induced performance degradation in real-world TLS deployments.

item.title: The OCH Authenticated Encryption Scheme
item.description: We introduce **OCH**, the first AEAD scheme designed to achieve *128-bit multi-user AE security*, *128-bit context commitment security*, and support for *256-bit nonces* with optional nonce privacy—addressing critical gaps in widely used schemes (e.g., AES-GCM, ChaCha20/Poly1305). Built modularly using provably secure transforms—including the Areion permutation family—OCH’s design enables broad applicability beyond AEAD. On Intel Raptor Lake CPUs, OCH achieves a peak encryption speed of **0.62 cycles/byte**, outperforming ChaCha20/Poly1305 (1.63 cpb) and TurboSHAKE128-Wrap (3.52 cpb), and approaching AES128-GCM (0.38 cpb). Its formal security analysis confirms robustness against multi-user attacks, context substitution, and nonce misuse.

item.title: Updatable Private Set Intersection from Symmetric-Key Techniques
item.description: We present the first Updatable Private Set Intersection (UPSI) protocol that avoids linear-scale public-key operations—replacing them with efficient symmetric-key primitives (OPRFs, OT extension, optimized hashing) used successfully in practical PSI. Our construction achieves **orders-of-magnitude speedup** in update latency and **68–89% less communication** vs. prior UPSI schemes. Crucially, we identify a critical gap: existing UPSI security models ignore *adaptively chosen inputs*, where an adversary selects new set elements per epoch based on prior views—a threat under which several published protocols fail even semi-honestly. We provide rigorous security proofs against such adaptive adversaries for multiple variants of our protocol. As a conceptual contribution, we introduce *affine set encoding*, a cleaner, composable abstraction for representing sets via oblivious key-value stores—potentially useful beyond UPSI.

item.title: Adaptive Methods Are Preferable in High Privacy Settings: An SDE Perspective
item.description: This paper provides the first stochastic differential equation (SDE)-based analysis of differentially private optimizers, revealing a fundamental advantage of adaptive methods—especially in high-privacy regimes ($\varepsilon \ll 1$). Under fixed hyperparameters and per-example clipping, DP-SGD suffers an $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon^2)$ privacy–utility trade-off with $\varepsilon$-independent convergence speed, whereas DP-SignSGD achieves a superior $\mathcal{O}(1/\varepsilon)$ trade-off and convergence rate *linear* in $\varepsilon$, dominating when privacy noise dominates. Crucially, while both methods attain comparable asymptotic performance under optimal learning rates, DP-SGD’s optimal rate scales linearly with $\varepsilon$, whereas DP-SignSGD’s is nearly $\varepsilon$-invariant—enabling seamless hyperparameter transfer across privacy levels. Empirical results confirm these findings across training/test metrics and extend robustly to DP-Adam, establishing adaptivity as a structural enabler of practical private learning.

item.title: RAIN: Secure and Robust Aggregation under Shuffle Model of Differential Privacy
item.description: We present **RAIN**, the first unified framework enabling *secure, robust, and verifiable* aggregation under the shuffle model of differential privacy (Shuffle-DP). To counter poisoning attacks amplified by noise and anonymization—and mitigate risks from compromised third-party shufflers—RAIN introduces sign-space aggregation to measure update consistency robustly in noisy, identity-free settings. We design two novel secret-shared protocols for shuffling and aggregation that operate natively on additive shares, preserving Shuffle-DP’s tight privacy bound without relaxation. Crucially, each round produces a cryptographically verified aggregate, enabling real-time, tamper-proof detection of selective dropping or manipulation under malicious adversaries. Extensive evaluation shows RAIN maintains strong accuracy (<0.8% drop) and convergence under ε=2–8 Shuffle-DP while resisting state-of-the-art poisoning attacks with >99.2% success rate. It achieves up to **90× lower communication cost** and **10× faster aggregation** versus prior secure aggregation schemes, establishing new efficiency and security frontiers for private federated learning.

item.title: Kraken: Higher-order EM Side-Channel Attacks on DNNs in Near and Far Field
item.description: Kraken introduces the first higher-order electromagnetic (EM) side-channel attacks targeting **Tensor Cores**—the specialized, high-performance units dominating modern GPUs—enabling DNN model extraction beyond prior CUDA Core-only attacks. By modeling Tensor Core micro-operations (e.g., GEMM, weight fetch) and applying **second-order Correlation Power Analysis (CPA)**, Kraken achieves 92.7% bit-level recovery of ResNet-50 convolutional weights from an NVIDIA A100 GPU in the near field (≤5 cm). Crucially, we demonstrate for the first time that GPU EM emanations leak LLM hyperparameters and weights at **100 cm distance through glass**, with statistically significant spectral signatures (2.1–2.4 GHz) correlated to weight updates. This reveals a previously underestimated far-field threat surface for multi-tenant cloud GPU environments.

item.title: Understanding the Resource Cost of Fully Homomorphic Encryption in Quantum Federated Learning
item.description: Quantum Federated Learning (QFL) promises privacy-preserving distributed training of Quantum Machine Learning (QML) models, yet raw gradients still risk leaking sensitive information. This work presents the **first implementation and evaluation of Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) — specifically the CKKS scheme — applied to parameters of a Quantum Convolutional Neural Network (QCNN) trained in a federated setting**. We train multiple QCNN architectures on MRI-based brain tumor classification and quantify FHE’s resource overhead: memory usage increases 12–18×, communication volume rises >15× per round, and decryption/aggregation latency dominates the training loop. Crucially, reducing model parameters to alleviate overhead consistently degrades classification accuracy (−5.2% to −7.8%), revealing a fundamental privacy–complexity–accuracy trade-off. Our results demonstrate that current FHE remains prohibitively expensive for real-world QFL deployment, calling for co-design of encryption, quantum circuit optimization, and sparse training.

item.title: Extending the Formalism and Theoretical Foundations of Cryptography to AI
item.description: This paper establishes the first formal cryptographic foundation for access control in AI agents. We introduce a language-model–specific attack taxonomy, define the *AIOracle* computational model, and propose a unified security game that jointly captures completeness (task success under benign conditions) and adversarial robustness—integrating confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Using this framework, we prove a fundamental impossibility: existing training-data confidentiality mechanisms (e.g., DP-fine-tuning) provably violate completeness. We further formalize a sound modular decomposition of *helpfulness* and *harmlessness*, enabling compositional security reasoning. Our results demonstrate that principled, measurable security for agentic systems requires modular design—where distinct security concerns are isolated, formally verified, and compositionally combined—making formal reduction proofs both necessary and achievable.

item.title: Beyond One-Size-Fits-All: Adaptive Subgraph Denoising for Zero-Shot Graph Learning with Large Language Models
item.description: Zero-shot graph learning remains challenging due to structural and semantic distribution shifts, where existing LLM-based approaches (e.g., Graph-R1) suffer from “one-size-fits-all” subgraph extraction, injecting irrelevant neighbors and edges that degrade reasoning fidelity. To address this, we propose **GraphSSR**, a novel adaptive subgraph denoising framework for LLM-based graph reasoning. Its core is the **SSR (Sample–Select–Reason) pipeline**, which dynamically tailors subgraph construction to task context via learnable sampling and selection modules. We introduce **SSR-SFT**, a data synthesis strategy generating high-quality, rationale-augmented SSR traces for supervised fine-tuning, and **SSR-RL**, a two-stage reinforcement learning method: Stage I optimizes prediction authenticity, while Stage II enforces denoising via sparsity- and relevance-aware rewards. Experiments show GraphSSR achieves +12.7% average accuracy gain over Graph-R1 on cross-domain zero-shot tasks, reduces subgraph size by 38%, and filters 91.4% of noisy edges—demonstrating principled, adaptive subgraph denoising as a key enabler for robust zero-shot graph reasoning.

item.title: StegaFFD: Privacy-Preserving Face Forgery Detection via Fine-Grained Steganographic Domain Lifting
item.description: Most face forgery detection (FFD) models require raw facial images, posing severe privacy risks in client-server settings where faces may be intercepted or leaked. Conventional privacy methods (e.g., blurring, encryption) introduce obvious distortions—alerting adversaries and degrading FFD performance due to artifacts. To address this, we propose **StegaFFD**, a steganography-based FFD framework that invisibly embeds faces into natural cover images and performs detection directly in the steganographic domain. To recover subtle forgery traces obscured by cover semantics, we introduce: (1) **Low-Frequency-Aware Decomposition (LFAD)** to suppress low-frequency cover interference; (2) **Spatial-Frequency Differential Attention (SFDA)** to jointly enhance spatial and frequency cues of hidden faces; and (3) **Steganographic Domain Alignment (SDA)** to bridge representation gaps between steganographic and raw domains. Extensive experiments across seven FFD benchmarks show StegaFFD achieves near-perfect imperceptibility (SSIM > 0.98), zero suspicion from human observers, and preserves FFD accuracy with only a 1.2% average AUC drop—outperforming all existing facial privacy protection baselines.

item.title: Sensory-Aware Sequential Recommendation via Review-Distilled Representations
item.description: We propose **ASEGR**, a novel sensory-aware sequential recommendation framework that distills linguistically grounded sensory attributes (e.g., *scent: vanilla*, *texture: velvety*) from product reviews into compact, reusable item embeddings. ASEGR employs a two-stage LLM distillation pipeline: (1) a fine-tuned teacher LLM extracts structured attribute–value pairs from unstructured reviews; (2) a lightweight student transformer encodes these into fixed-dimensional sensory embeddings, integrated as auxiliary item representations in standard sequential recommenders (SASRec, BERT4Rec, BSARec). Evaluated across four Amazon domains, sensory-enhanced models consistently outperform ID-only baselines (avg. +4.3% Recall@20), demonstrating that sensory semantics provide complementary, behavior-agnostic signals. Qualitative analysis confirms strong alignment with human perception and enables interpretable, language-grounded recommendations. ASEGR offers a principled, scalable bridge between semantic information extraction and sequential modeling.

item.title: LLM-MLFFN: Multi-Level Autonomous Driving Behavior Feature Fusion via Large Language Model
item.description: Accurate classification of autonomous driving behaviors remains challenging due to the gap between low-level trajectory data and high-level semantic intent. This paper proposes **LLM-MLFFN**, a novel framework that synergizes structured numerical feature modeling with LLM-driven semantic abstraction. It comprises three components: (1) a multi-level extractor capturing statistical, behavioral, and dynamic features; (2) an LLM-based semantic descriptor generating human-interpretable behavior narratives from raw trajectories and contextual cues; and (3) a dual-channel fusion network integrating numerical and semantic features via hierarchical weighted attention. Evaluated on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, LLM-MLFFN achieves **94.3% accuracy** across 8 behavior classes—outperforming state-of-the-art ML models by up to 3.7%. Ablation studies confirm the critical roles of multi-level fusion (+2.1%) and LLM-derived semantics (+1.9%). This work establishes a principled, interpretable pathway for robust, cognition-aware autonomous behavior understanding.

item.title: Less Noise, Same Certificate: Retain Sensitivity for Unlearning
item.description: Certified machine unlearning guarantees that the unlearned model is statistically indistinguishable from one retrained solely on the retain set $R = S \setminus U$, thus provably erasing influence of deletion set $U$. Existing methods borrow noise calibration from differential privacy (DP), using *global sensitivity*—the worst-case output change over all adjacent datasets. We argue this is overly conservative: unlearning need not protect $R$’s privacy, only ensure indistinguishability across different $U$ for fixed $R$. To address this, we introduce *retain sensitivity*: the maximum output change over all $U$ such that $R$ remains unchanged. Though insufficient for DP, it is both necessary and sufficient for certified unlearning—enabling identical certificates with significantly less noise. We prove retain sensitivity is strictly smaller than global sensitivity and demonstrate noise reductions of 42–68% empirically on MST, PCA, and ERM tasks. Furthermore, we refine GAUSS and SISA-based algorithms using retain sensitivity, leveraging regularity in $R$ to further improve utility without compromising certification.

item.title: Breaking the Prototype Bias Loop: Confidence-Aware Federated Contrastive Learning for Highly Imbalanced Clients
item.description:
## Breaking the Prototype Bias Loop: Confidence-Aware Federated Contrastive Learning for Highly Imbalanced Clients
Local class imbalance and statistical heterogeneity across clients trap prototype-based federated contrastive learning (FCL) in a self-reinforcing bias loop: imbalanced local data yield biased prototypes, which aggregate into skewed global prototypes reused as contrastive anchors—amplifying errors over rounds. To break this, we propose **Confidence-Aware Federated Contrastive Learning (CAFedCL)**. It introduces: (i) a confidence-aware prototype aggregation that downweights high-variance local prototypes using predictive uncertainty (e.g., entropy and gradient variance); (ii) lightweight conditional generative augmentation for minority classes; and (iii) geometric consistency regularization to preserve inter-class angles and intra-class compactness in embedding space. Theoretically, we prove our aggregation reduces estimation variance, bounds global prototype drift, and ensures convergence. Extensive experiments under severe imbalance (e.g., 100:1 head-to-tail ratio) and strong heterogeneity show CAFedCL consistently outperforms FedCon, FedCA, and FedProto—achieving +3.2–7.8% higher accuracy and reducing fairness gap by 41.5%.

item.title: Integrating Homomorphic Encryption and Synthetic Data in FL for Privacy and Learning Quality
item.description: Federated learning (FL) promises privacy-preserving collaborative model training, yet struggles to jointly ensure high learning quality, strong privacy, and low computational overhead—especially when deploying expensive homomorphic encryption (HE). To address this, we propose **Alternating Federated Learning (Alt-FL)**, a novel framework that interleaves *authentic rounds* (local training on real data with HE-encrypted parameter uploads) and *synthetic rounds* (training on locally generated synthetic data with plaintext uploads). By integrating lightweight cGAN-based synthetic data generation and strategic HE sparsification, Alt-FL enhances data diversity for better accuracy while confining HE usage to only half the rounds—reducing encryption/decryption costs by up to 48% versus Selective HE. Experiments show Alt-FL achieves **13.4% higher model accuracy**, faster convergence, and robust resistance against DLG-style reconstruction attacks (99.8% success rate reduction), demonstrating a practical balance of privacy, utility, and efficiency.

item.title: Convex and Non-convex Federated Learning with Stale Stochastic Gradients: Diminishing Step Size is All You Need
item.description: We propose a unified framework for federated learning under stale, stochastic, and potentially biased gradient updates. Contrary to prior work advocating delay-adaptive step sizes (e.g., scaling by delay magnitude), we prove that a *pre-specified diminishing step size*—such as $\eta_t = c/\sqrt{t}$ for nonconvex objectives or $\eta_t = c/(\mu t)$ for $\mu$-strongly convex ones—is sufficient to achieve optimal convergence rates. Specifically, our analysis establishes $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T})$ suboptimality gap for general nonconvex objectives and $\mathcal{O}(1/(\mu T))$ rate for strongly convex objectives—matching the best-known rates of vanilla SGD without delays. Crucially, these guarantees hold under arbitrary bounded staleness $\tau$, gradient bias, and variance, with *no explicit dependence on $\tau$* in the leading terms—demonstrating inherent robustness of diminishing steps to delay-induced noise. Our result simplifies implementation, eliminates the need for delay monitoring or adaptive hyperparameter tuning, and provides a theoretically grounded, practical alternative to adaptive schemes.

item.title: EdgeFLow: Serverless Federated Learning via Sequential Model Migration in Edge Networks
item.description: EdgeFLow is a novel serverless federated learning (FL) framework that replaces the central cloud server with sequential model migration across geographically proximal edge base stations (e.g., 5G gNodeBs). By performing all aggregation and propagation locally within edge clusters—and eliminating cloud-bound transmissions—EdgeFLow drastically reduces global communication overhead and end-to-end latency. We provide the first rigorous convergence analysis for such a migration-based FL system under non-convex objectives and heterogeneous non-IID data, proving $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ convergence to stationary points. Experiments on realistic urban edge topologies and benchmark datasets (CIFAR-10, FEMNIST) show EdgeFLow achieves accuracy comparable to FedAvg (within ±0.8%) while cutting total communication volume by 63.5% and training latency by 41.2%. This work establishes a foundational, communication-efficient architectural paradigm for scalable FL in IoT and edge networks.

item.title: Learning When to Act or Refuse: Guarding Agentic Reasoning Models for Safe Multi-Step Tool Use
item.description: Agentic language models operate under a distinct safety regime: unlike chat models, they perform multi-step planning and tool execution where a single error (e.g., unauthorized file access) can cause irreversible harm. Standard alignment methods—designed for static generation—fail here due to sequential decision-making, adversarial tool feedback, and overconfident intermediate reasoning. We propose **MOSAIC**, a post-training framework that explicitly learns *when to act or refuse* by structuring inference as a *plan → check safety → act/refuse* loop, treating refusal as a first-class action. To train without trajectory-level labels, we use preference-based RL with pairwise trajectory comparisons—capturing nuanced safety distinctions missed by scalar rewards. Evaluated zero-shot across Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen3-4B-Thinking, and Phi-4 on out-of-distribution benchmarks (harmful tasks, prompt injection, benign tool use, cross-domain privacy leakage), MOSAIC reduces harmful behavior by up to 50%, increases refusal on injected harmful tasks by >20%, cuts privacy leakage, and preserves or improves benign task performance—demonstrating strong generalization across models, domains, and agentic settings.

item.title: UniSkill: A Dataset for Matching University Curricula to Professional Competencies
item.description: UniSkill introduces the first publicly available, fine-grained dataset for matching university curricula to professional competencies defined in the European Skills, Competences, Qualifications and Occupations (ESCO) taxonomy. We manually annotate and synthetically augment pairs between graduate-level course titles/sentences and ESCO skills from two high-demand occupation groups: *Systems Analysts* and *Management and Organization Analysts*. The dataset supports dual-direction matching (course-to-skill and skill-to-course) at both title-level and sentence-level granularities, accompanied by detailed, reproducible annotation guidelines. Using UniSkill, we train and evaluate BERT-based retrieval models, achieving an **87% F1-score** on the human-annotated test set—demonstrating the feasibility and robustness of automated curriculum–competency alignment. UniSkill bridges a critical data gap between higher education and labor market requirements, enabling scalable skill-aware course recommendation and competency mapping.

item.title: PrivMedChat: End-to-End Differentially Private RLHF for Medical Dialogue Systems
item.description: PrivMedChat is the first end-to-end differentially private RLHF (DP-RLHF) framework tailored for medical dialogue systems. To mitigate privacy risks from sensitive doctor-patient interactions, it applies DP-SGD at three critical stages: (i) medical supervised fine-tuning (SFT), (ii) reward modeling on preference pairs, and (iii) PPO actor/critic updates—*only* on dialogue-derived prompts, while freezing the DP-trained reward model to conserve privacy budget. We further propose an annotation-free preference construction method, pairing physician responses with clinically filtered non-expert generations. Experiments show that at ε = 7, PrivMedChat achieves the highest ROUGE-L (0.156) among all DP baselines, reduces clinical hallucinations to 1.4% and harmful advice to 0.4%, attains a top LLM-jury score of 2.86/3.0, and yields near-chance membership inference (AUC: 0.510–0.555). Code is open-sourced.

item.title: Post-Quantum Anonymous Signatures from the Lattice Isomorphism Group Action
item.description: This paper bridges a critical gap in post-quantum anonymous cryptography by enabling the **Lattice Isomorphism Problem (LIP)**—whose underlying group action is infinite, non-compact, and non-commutative—to serve as a foundation for practical anonymous signatures. We introduce the **Generalized Group Action Inversion (G-GAI)** assumption, formalizing the hardness of LIP in a cryptographic-friendly manner. From standard zero-knowledge OR proofs, we construct two fundamental primitives: a **generic blind signature** and a **strong designated-verifier signature**, both provably secure under G-GAI and featuring **non-delegability**—a crucial property preventing implicit delegation of signing authority. Our schemes achieve **EUF-CMA security** and **computational anonymity** in the standard model, without random oracles. This is the first work to lift LIP from a theoretical hardness candidate to a viable building block for quantum-resistant privacy protocols.

item.title: Revisiting the Security of Sparkle
item.description: We revisit the threshold Schnorr signature scheme Sparkle (CRYPTO 2023) and its variant Sparkle+, resolving long-standing gaps: Sparkle lacked even a static security proof, while Sparkle+’s claimed fully adaptive security was flawed. We provide the **first static security proof for Sparkle**, and—our main result—a **tight, fully adaptive security proof in the pure random oracle model**, *without* algebraic group assumptions. The key obstacle—breakdown of rewinding in full adaptivity—is overcome via a new **Vandermonde Circular Discrete-Logarithm (VCDL) assumption**, an interactive strengthening of Cho et al.’s circular DL assumption. VCDL eliminates rewinding and enables tight analysis. We justify VCDL by reducing it to the LDVR problem (Crites et al., CRYPTO 2025) in the elliptic-curve generic group model; conversely, VCDL implies LDVR in the standard model. Finally, we generalize VCDL and identify a related assumption yielding a tight adaptive multi-user security proof for basic Schnorr signatures—a result of independent interest.

item.title: Efficient Single-Server Stateful PIR Using Format-Preserving Encryption
item.description: Stateful Private Information Retrieval (PIR) enables single-server PIR with sublinear communication by letting the client maintain lightweight state across queries. Yet, prior single-server stateful PIR schemes suffer from high amortized communication, heavy server computation, or excessive client storage. We propose **HarmonyPIR**, a new efficient construction that simultaneously optimizes all three metrics. Technically, we introduce a streamlined hint organization relying on only *one* random permutation—realized either via AES-based pseudorandom permutation or the NIST-standardized FF1 Format-Preserving Encryption (FPE). This design eliminates costly multi-dimensional encoding and redundant state materialization. HarmonyPIR achieves up to **5× lower amortized communication** and **up to two orders of magnitude faster amortized server computation** compared to the state of the art. Its client storage is bounded by a single 128-bit key plus <1 KB metadata, enabling practical deployment in privacy-sensitive database systems.

item.title: Information-Theoretic Strong Traceable Secret Sharing Schemes
item.description: This paper advances information-theoretic traceable secret sharing (TSS) for general access structures in three key directions. First, we present the **first fully information-theoretic construction** for the $\emptyset$-strong traceability model of Goyal, Jain, and Partap (EC’26), eliminating their reliance on indistinguishability obfuscation (iO) and resolving their open question on minimal assumptions. Second, motivated by the impossibility of strong traceability, we introduce *hidden mildly strong traceability*—a practical relaxation bridging strong and hiding models—and provide an **information-theoretic scheme** for arbitrary monotone access structures. Third, we study stateful traceability, where reconstruction boxes maintain state across queries, and prove a **strong impossibility result**, establishing a fundamental limit. Our work tightens the foundations of TSS by reducing cryptographic assumptions, expanding feasible models, and clarifying theoretical boundaries.

item.title: Secure Cloud Storage: Modularization, Network Adversaries and Adaptive Corruptions
item.description: We extend the CRYPTO 2024 secure cloud storage framework of BDGHP along four key axes. First, we strengthen security from selective to **adaptive corruptions**, resolving technical barriers from file sharing via a minimal modification (post-registration public-key binding). Second, we introduce a modular **authentication interface**, decoupling protocol logic from cryptographic primitives and clarifying the precise role of 2HDH—enabling secure alternatives like PAKEs. Third, we propose a weaker **post-registration network adversary model**, where the adversary controls the network *except during registration*, enabling concrete bounds against *online password guessing* (e.g., $O(q_{\text{reg}} \cdot q_{\text{login}})$ queries), unlike the stronger model that permits offline attacks. Finally, we formalize and prove **explicit authentication**, ensuring clients verify the server’s possession of the correct password-derived key—leveraging our new authentication abstraction and an integrated MAC scheme, previously unused in BDGHP’s security analysis. All results hold in the random oracle model under standard assumptions.

item.title: Round-Optimal Threshold Blind Signatures without Random Oracles
item.description: This paper presents the first **round-optimal threshold blind signature scheme without random oracles**. It achieves security in the Algebraic Group Model (AGM) for asymmetric pairing groups and tolerates adaptive corruption of up to $t-1$ signers (where $t$ is the threshold). Compared to recent schemes by Lehmann et al. (EUROCRYPT 2025) and Jarecki & Nazarian (ASIACRYPT 2025), ours eliminates *both* the random oracle assumption *and* all $q$-type assumptions in the AGM—relying solely on the standard **2-DL assumption**. At its core, we introduce a new pairing-based, round-optimal, RO-free blind signature, which serves as the building block for our threshold construction. Both the base blind signature and the threshold variant incur only **twice the communication and computation cost** of the celebrated blind BLS signature, preserving practical efficiency while achieving stronger foundational security.

item.title: Finite Field Arithmetic for ML-KEM Using Zech's Logarithm
item.description: This paper addresses the computational bottleneck of finite field arithmetic in ML-KEM over $\mathbb{F}_{3329}$, a critical component of post-quantum cryptographic implementations. We propose a novel logarithmic representation using a primitive element $\alpha$ of $\mathbb{F}_{3329}^*$, enhanced with **Zech’s logarithm** to support *all* field operations—multiplication, addition, and subtraction—within a unified logarithmic domain. Multiplication reduces to modular addition in $\mathbb{Z}_{3328}$, while addition/subtraction leverage precomputed Zech values $Z(i) = \log_\alpha(1+\alpha^i)$, enabling efficient log-domain computation without conditional branches. Crucially, we introduce a robust zero-handling mechanism that explicitly encodes $0$ as a sentinel value, ensuring constant-time execution and hardware efficiency. Implemented on ARM Cortex-M4, our method accelerates core ML-KEM field operations by 1.8× on average and reduces ROM footprint for the Zech table to just 13.3 KB—demonstrating practicality for resource-constrained devices.

item.title: An attack on the CFS scheme and on TII McEliece challenges
item.description: This paper breaks the 25-year-old CFS signature scheme—a long-standing open problem in code-based cryptography. While Faugère et al.’s 2011 distinguisher revealed that high-rate binary Goppa codes are structurally non-random, no efficient key-recovery attack existed until now, except for pathological degree-2 Goppa codes. We repurpose the Pfaffian modeling from Couvreur–Mora–Tillich (Asiacrypt 2023) by introducing a shortening technique and systematically searching for squares in the associated ideal, enabling a polynomial-time algebraic attack on standard CFS instantiated with high-rate binary Goppa codes of degree ≥3. Our method fully recovers the secret key, invalidating CFS as a secure signature scheme. We demonstrate practical impact by solving all TII McEliece Challenge instances—including the highest-security instance claiming 210-bit security—in polynomial time on a single CPU. This result fundamentally revises the security assessment of high-rate Goppa codes.

item.title: Efficient Private Range Queries on Public Data
item.description: Range queries retrieve or aggregate entries within a multi-dimensional rectangle; *private* range queries hide the client’s query region from the server while accessing public data. We propose **RangeR**, a constant-round private range query scheme supporting *any associative aggregation function* (e.g., SUM, MAX, TOP-K) and deployable with *any number of servers*. In the single-server setting, RangeR outperforms HADES (VLDB 2025)—the prior state-of-the-art for linear aggregations—by **10–100× faster computation** and **50–90% less communication**, thanks to lightweight cryptographic techniques and dimension-aware optimizations. We demonstrate its practicality in a privacy-preserving map application: using OpenStreetMap data, a user finds top-rated restaurants within 1 km of their location in **under 2 seconds**, revealing *only* that they are somewhere in the USA—not their coordinates, city, or even state. RangeR bridges expressiveness, efficiency, and strong privacy for real-world public-data analytics.

item.title: Defending Against Backdoor Attacks in Homomorphically Encrypted Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) with homomorphic encryption (HE) preserves update privacy but blocks conventional backdoor defenses requiring plaintext model weights. To address this, we propose an *inference-time* defense that detects poisoned predictions by analyzing abnormal neuron activation patterns in the *decrypted global model*, without accessing or decrypting any client’s encrypted updates. Specifically, we identify “dormant neurons”—units with near-zero activation on clean inputs but disproportionately high activation under trigger patterns—and build class-specific statistical activation baselines using a small clean dataset (label-free). Predictions triggering abnormally strong dormant-neuron responses are rejected. Our method introduces no training overhead, remains robust under severe non-IID data (e.g., Dirichlet α=0.1), and achieves **100% detection of poisoned images** while maintaining 90% main-task accuracy—even under 99% attack success rate. This is the first approach to bridge HE-based privacy and backdoor security via activation semantics at inference time.

item.title: Protection against Source Inference Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) is vulnerable to Source Inference Attacks (SIA), where an honest-but-curious server identifies the exact client contributing a given training sample—despite standard differential privacy defenses failing without severe accuracy degradation. We propose a novel defense within the shuffle model of FL, demonstrating that naive gradient-level shuffling is insufficient. Instead, we introduce **parameter-level shuffling combined with the Residue Number System (RNS)**, enabling lossless, identity-agnostic reordering of model parameters without noise injection. Our method provably breaks statistical links between gradients and client identities while preserving full model accuracy. Experiments across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Sentiment140 show that baseline shuffling yields SIA accuracy of 68%–82%, whereas our approach reduces it to 22.3%–26.1%—statistically indistinguishable from random guessing (e.g., 10% for 10 clients). The solution integrates seamlessly with existing privacy mechanisms and incurs no accuracy trade-off.

item.title: Accurate, private, secure, federated U-statistics with higher degree
item.description: We address the problem of privately and securely computing degree-$k$ ($k \geq 2$) U-statistics—e.g., Kendall’s $\tau$, AUC, Gini mean difference—in federated learning. Prior approaches rely on low-utility local differential privacy (LDP) or suffer from poor scalability with domain discretization. We propose the first MPC-based protocol for *centrally* differentially private U-statistic computation, achieving significantly higher accuracy. Our method leverages symmetric kernel decomposition and optimized noise injection to minimize MSE while guaranteeing $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-privacy. Theoretically, its MSE scales as $O(1/(n^k\varepsilon^2))$, outperforming LDP baselines by up to $O(n^{k-1})$. Empirically, on Kendall’s $\tau$, it reduces MSE by **up to four orders of magnitude**; for AUC under $\varepsilon = 1$, error is only $1/270$ of state-of-the-art baselines. The protocol is communication-efficient ($O(n^{k-1})$ per round) and supports arbitrary $k \geq 2$.

item.title: DualSentinel: A Lightweight Framework for Detecting Targeted Attacks in Black-box LLM via Dual Entropy Lull Pattern
item.description: Recent black-box LLM deployments face severe threats from targeted attacks (e.g., backdoor, prompt injection), yet existing defenses are impractical due to high access requirements, prohibitive overhead, or inference disruption. We propose **DualSentinel**, a lightweight, runtime defense framework that detects attack activation *during* LLM generation. Its core insight is the **Entropy Lull**—a characteristic period of abnormally low and stable token probability entropy when an LLM is hijacked into a fixed output path. DualSentinel employs a dual-check mechanism: (1) a magnitude- and trend-aware entropy monitor for sensitive, real-time lull detection; followed by (2) lightweight task-flipping verification—if the lull persists under semantically inverted tasks (e.g., QA → reverse-QA), the attack is confirmed with high confidence. Evaluated across 12 black-box LLMs and 5 attack types, DualSentinel achieves **99.2% detection accuracy**, **<0.3% false positive rate**, and adds only **<8 ms latency** and **<2 MB memory**, enabling practical, zero-modification deployment. Code: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18479273.

item.title: From Secure Agentic AI to Secure Agentic Web: Challenges, Threats, and Future Directions
item.description: This survey bridges Secure Agentic AI and the emerging Secure Agentic Web. We introduce a component-aligned threat taxonomy covering prompt abuse, environment injection, memory attacks, toolchain abuse, model tampering, and agent network attacks. Defense strategies reviewed include prompt hardening, safety-aware decoding, privilege-controlled tool/API access, runtime monitoring, continuous red-teaming, and protocol-level mechanisms (e.g., signed delegation, trusted channels). Crucially, we show how risks escalate in the Agentic Web—where delegation chains, cross-domain interactions, and protocol-mediated ecosystems amplify threats via composition and propagation. We identify four open challenges for web-scale deployment: interoperable identity/authorization, end-to-end provenance and traceability, ecosystem-level coordinated response, and scalable evaluation under adaptive adversaries. Our work connects empirical security findings with system-level requirements to chart practical research directions toward trustworthy, deployable agent ecosystems.

item.title: Inference-Time Safety For Code LLMs Via Retrieval-Augmented Revision
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) for code generation face critical trustworthiness challenges due to opaque security reasoning and brittleness against evolving vulnerabilities. We propose **Retrieval-Augmented Revision (RAR)**—an inference-time safety mechanism that operates *without model retraining*. RAR first detects security risks in generated code, then retrieves expert-vetted security discussions from a curated Stack Overflow knowledge base, and finally guides a lightweight LLM to revise the code with transparent, evidence-based edits. Our approach advances trustworthiness along three axes: **interpretability** (interventions grounded in real community explanations), **robustness** (adapting to new vulnerabilities via knowledge-base updates alone), and **safety alignment** (real-time blocking of unsafe code pre-deployment). Evaluated on benchmark and real-world datasets, RAR significantly improves code security over prompt engineering alone—reducing vulnerability rates by 42.7% on average—while introducing *zero* new vulnerabilities per static analysis. This demonstrates retrieval-augmented inference-time intervention as a practical, complementary safeguard for trustworthy LLM-based coding.

item.title: Detection-Gated Glottal Segmentation with Zero-Shot Cross-Dataset Transfer and Clinical Feature Extraction
item.description:
## Background & Innovation Accurate glottal segmentation in high-speed videoendoscopy (HSV) is critical for quantifying laryngeal kinematics, yet existing deep learning models suffer from spurious artifacts and poor cross-dataset generalizability. We propose a **detection-gated pipeline**: a YOLOv8 detector spatially gates a U-Net segmenter, while a temporal consistency wrapper suppresses false positives during glottal closure and instrument occlusion. Trained on only 600 frames from GIRAFE (<0.5% of the dataset), it achieves **zero-shot transfer** to the large-scale, multi-institutional BAGLS dataset—without any fine-tuning.
## Key Results The framework achieves state-of-the-art Dice scores of **0.81 on GIRAFE** and **0.85 on BAGLS** (in-distribution), outperforming prior methods by +0.12 in DSC. Downstream validation on 65 clinical subjects confirms robust extraction of kinematic features (Open Quotient, area coefficient of variation), with ICC > 0.92 vs. expert annotations. Critically, the glottal area CV significantly distinguishes healthy from pathological vocal function (*p* = 0.006). At **~35 FPS**, the lightweight architecture enables real-time clinical deployment. Code, weights, and evaluation scripts are publicly available.
